naturalMeaning: The Russian New Year gift-giver — a distinct figure from Santa Claus, not a stand-in for him. See the culture notes for how he differs in look, lore, and timing.

examples.text: Дед Мороз приносит подарки в новогоднюю ночь.
examples.romaji: Dyed Marós prinósit padárki v novogódnyuyu noch'.
examples.translation: Ded Moroz brings gifts on New Year's Eve.

translation: Snegurochka, the Snow Maiden (Ded Moroz's granddaughter)
naturalMeaning: Ded Moroz's granddaughter and helper — a character unique to Russian New Year tradition with no equivalent in the Santa Claus story.

examples.text: Дед Мороз всегда приходит вместе со Снегурочкой.
examples.romaji: Dyed Marós vsyegdá prikhódit vmyéste sa Snegúrochkoy.
examples.translation: Ded Moroz always comes together with Snegurochka.

naturalMeaning: Ded Moroz's iconic long blue or red fur coat with silver trim — not the red-and-white suit Santa Claus wears.
Traditional Russian felted wool boots — part of Ded Moroz's classic winter look, plural-only word (no meaningful singular in everyday use).
The decorated fir tree at the center of the celebration — always called the ёлка (New Year tree), never a 'Christmas tree,' since it's tied to Новый год rather than Christmas in Russian tradition.

examples.text: Мы поставили ёлку в углу комнаты.
examples.romaji: My pastávili yólku v uglú kómnaty.
examples.translation: We put the tree up in the corner of the room.

naturalMeaning: The seasonal outdoor markets, set up in city squares and parking lots every December, where families pick out and buy their live ёлка.
The near-universal New Year dish — a mayonnaise-dressed salad of diced potato, carrot, egg, pickle, peas, and bologna-style sausage. See the culture notes for its history.

examples.text: Без салата Оливье это не Новый год.
examples.romaji: Byez salátа Ol'ivyé eto nye Nóvy god.
examples.translation: It's not New Year without Olivier salad.

naturalMeaning: Drunk at the stroke of midnight, timed to the chimes — the single most important drink of the night.
Strongly tied to the holiday's smell and memory for most Russians — see the culture notes on why the scent alone evokes New Year.

translation: herring under a fur coat (layered beet-and-herring salad)
naturalMeaning: A layered salad of herring, potato, carrot, egg, and beet under mayonnaise — the beet gives it a deep purple 'fur coat.' Almost as common on the New Year table as Olivier salad.
Russia's single biggest holiday of the year, a legacy of the Soviet era suppressing religious Christmas celebration. See the culture notes.
The Kremlin's Spasskaya Tower clock, whose midnight chimes (broadcast live nationwide) mark the exact start of the New Year — plural-only word.

translation: the striking of the Kremlin chimes

examples.text: Все ждут боя курантов в полночь.
examples.romaji: Fsye zhdut bóya kuránta f pólnach'.
examples.translation: Everyone waits for the striking of the chimes at midnight.

naturalMeaning: Traditionally done during the twelve strikes of the chimes — see the culture notes on the paper-and-champagne wish ritual.
Overlaps with фейерверк but leans toward large, official/public displays rather than small hand-held ones.
A short televised speech broadcast in the final minutes before midnight, watched nationwide as a fixed part of the countdown ritual.
A charming coda to the holiday season, caused by the 13-day gap between the Julian and Gregorian calendars — see the culture notes.
Russia has an official multi-day public holiday period starting January 1st; каникулы is plural-only, used for school breaks generally as well as this period.

examples.text: С Новым годом! Здоровья и счастья!
examples.romaji: S Nóvym gódom! Zdaróv'ya i schást'ya!
examples.translation: Happy New Year! Health and happiness!

lemma: С Новым годом, с новым счастьем!
romaji: S Nóvym gódom, s nóvym schást'yem!
translation: Happy New Year, happy new happiness!
naturalMeaning: A longer, more traditional/folksy variant of the standard greeting, often used by older relatives.
Used the way English uses 'happy [holiday]' — Поздравляю с Новым годом! literally 'I congratulate [you] on the New Year!'

translation: "The Irony of Fate, or Enjoy Your Bath!" (1975 film)
naturalMeaning: The beloved 1975 Eldar Ryazanov film watched by much of the country every New Year's Eve — a genuine shared cultural touchstone. See the culture notes.

A genuine, older greeting said to someone after they've been to the banya (steam bath) — now inseparably linked to the film title and often used playfully around New Year's Eve, since the film's plot begins with a banya trip.

A long-running genre of New Year's Eve television variety show — musical performances, comedy sketches, and celebrity toasts, a fixture of the night's viewing alongside the president's address and 'The Irony of Fate.'

translation: round dance (dancing in a circle around the tree)
naturalMeaning: Children traditionally join hands and circle the ёлка singing New Year songs — a staple of both school and family celebrations.

translation: "A Little Fir Tree Was Born in the Forest" (classic New Year song)
naturalMeaning: The best-known Russian New Year song, sung by children circling the ёлка in a хоровод — nearly every Russian knows it from childhood.
